title: Theory of Change for Scale Welfare: East Asian Fish Welfare

nodes.title: Develop welfare training programs for target species
nodes.text: Creation of species-specific protocols and training materials for tilapia and milkfish welfare improvements

connections.evidence: FWI has developed welfare improvement approaches, but CE research emphasizes this work is 'unprecedented' and 'co-founders will have to generate your own research, run your own tests' (CE Report, p.30). Research notes difficulty identifying 'the how' of water quality improvements (CE Report, p.4).
connections.assumptions: Effective training programs can be developed despite limited existing knowledge base. Local farming contexts allow for practical application of welfare principles developed elsewhere.

nodes.title: Conduct pilot welfare projects with partner farmers
nodes.text: Direct implementation of welfare improvements on selected farms to test approaches and generate evidence

connections.evidence: FWI's farmer-level approach has shown tractability, with impact on 1.84M fish as of 2024 (Fish Welfare Initiative, 2024). CE research notes farmer-level work provides 'great information value' about conditions and implementable improvements (CE Report, p.17).
connections.assumptions: Farmers will be willing to participate in pilot projects. Pilot results will be representative of broader farmer population and applicable across different farm contexts.

nodes.title: Publish research showing economic benefits of welfare improvements
nodes.text: Cost-benefit analyses and case studies showing financial viability of welfare improvements for farmers

connections.evidence: CE research notes reduced stocking densities 'may have benefits of increased survival and lower disease susceptibility' creating 'win-win for farmers and fish' (CE Report, p.9). However, research acknowledges uncertainty about economic impacts and warns improvements could help 'proliferate the industry' (CE Report, p.32).
connections.assumptions: Welfare improvements will show measurable economic benefits under real-world conditions. Economic benefits will be sufficient to motivate farmer behavior change without external incentives.

nodes.title: Build relationships with equipment suppliers and corporate partners
nodes.text: Establishing supply chains and market connections to support welfare implementation

connections.evidence: FWI has secured corporate commitments from SAGE Organics and Fipola (Fish Welfare Initiative, 2021b; 2022b). CE research notes corporate approach may be promising but uncertain due to different market structures in developing countries with more middlemen (CE Report, p.18).
connections.assumptions: Equipment suppliers exist and can be convinced to serve welfare-focused market. Corporate partners will be willing to engage despite limited consumer demand for fish welfare in target regions.

nodes.title: Welfare improvement programs scale to reach farmers
nodes.text: Training programs exist, are delivered to farmers, and provide practical knowledge for welfare improvements

connections.evidence: CE research emphasizes this is 'explore' phase work with limited precedent. Research warns that 'there is not yet an obvious, tested, and scalable ask and approach to improving fish welfare' (CE Report, p.3). Training effectiveness depends on local context that is largely unknown.
connections.assumptions: Training can be effectively delivered across language and cultural barriers. Farmers have time and motivation to participate in training programs. Training leads to actual knowledge retention and application.

nodes.title: Research shows economic viability of welfare improvements
nodes.text: Credible evidence exists showing that welfare improvements provide financial benefits to farmers

connections.evidence: CE research suggests potential for 'win-win' outcomes but acknowledges uncertainty. Research notes improving water quality 'can be quite difficult' and may increase carrying capacity, potentially leading to more fish produced (CE Report, p.32). Economic benefits remain largely theoretical.
connections.assumptions: Welfare improvements will consistently provide economic benefits rather than imposing costs. Benefits will be sufficient to outweigh implementation costs and farmer risk aversion.

nodes.title: Supply chains for welfare equipment and market demand are created
nodes.text: Equipment and services needed for welfare improvements are accessible to farmers with market incentives in place

connections.evidence: CE research notes importance of equipment access for water quality management including aerators (CE Report, p.8). However, research acknowledges uncertainty about corporate market structures in developing countries with 'much more informal/traditional commerce' (CE Report, p.18).
connections.assumptions: Supply chains can be developed to serve small-scale farmers cost-effectively. Market demand will be sufficient to sustain supply chain development.

connections.evidence: CE research notes importance of equipment access for water quality management including aerators (CE Report, p.8). However, research acknowledges uncertainty about corporate market structures in developing countries with 'much more informal/traditional commerce' (CE Report, p.18).
connections.assumptions: Supply chains can be developed to serve small-scale farmers cost-effectively. Market demand will be sufficient to sustain supply chain development.

nodes.title: Farmers have technical knowledge and equipment to improve conditions
nodes.text: Farmers understand water quality parameters and have tools to monitor and improve conditions

connections.evidence: CE research emphasizes this is 'explore' phase work with limited precedent for integrated husbandry training. Water quality management 'can be quite difficult' and stocking density decisions require balancing welfare with economic pressures. Training effectiveness across language/cultural barriers for complex technical practices remains unproven.
connections.assumptions: Training can effectively cover both water quality monitoring and stocking density management. Farmers can retain and apply complex technical knowledge covering multiple welfare parameters. Training leads to sustained behavior change in established farming practices.

nodes.title: Farmers believe economic benefits of improved welfare practices
nodes.text: Farmers are convinced that welfare improvements will enhance farm profitability through reduced mortality and disease

connections.evidence: CE research suggests welfare improvements could provide 'increased survival and lower disease susceptibility' through reduced stocking and better water quality, creating potential 'win-win for farmers and fish' (CE Report, p.9). However, economic benefits remain largely theoretical and farmer willingness to change established practices is uncertain, especially when improvements may reduce total production volume.
connections.assumptions: Economic benefits of integrated husbandry practices will be clear and immediate enough to motivate sustained behavior change. Farmers have capacity to invest in both water quality equipment and accept lower stocking densities. Reduced mortality compensates for potential production volume decreases.

nodes.title: Market incentives reward higher welfare practices with higher prices
nodes.text: Economic incentives exist that make welfare improvements financially attractive to farmers

connections.evidence: CE research notes limited consumer support for fish welfare especially in developing countries, and corporate structures with many middlemen reduce influence on farm practices (CE Report, p.18). Market incentives for integrated welfare practices (both water quality and stocking density) would require premium markets that may not be accessible to small-scale farmers.
connections.assumptions: Market premiums can be developed for comprehensive welfare practices despite limited consumer demand. Corporate commitments will translate into meaningful incentives for farmers to adopt both water quality and stocking density improvements. Premium markets will be accessible to small-scale farmers rather than just large producers.

nodes.title: Farmers consistently implement better husbandry practices
nodes.text: Farmers maintain appropriate stocking density limits for different species while consistently implementing water quality management practices that keep parameters (dissolved oxygen, ammonia, pH, temperature) within welfare-appropriate ranges.

connections.evidence: CE research identifies both water quality as 'most important component of ensuring good fish welfare' and high stocking densities as causing 'increased mortalities, high stress, higher rates of injuries, increased aggression' (CE Report, p.8-9). FWI has successfully integrated both elements into welfare standards. Multiple sources confirm these practices directly impact fish stress, mortality, and disease susceptibility (Cooke, 2016; MacIntyre et al., 2008).
connections.assumptions: Farmers can effectively manage both water quality and stocking density simultaneously without one undermining the other. Integrated husbandry practices are sustainable without constant external support. Improvements don't inadvertently shift production to other farms or create new welfare problems.

nodes.title: Measurable improvements in farmed fish welfare
nodes.text: Farmed fish in East Asia experience reduced chronic suffering and improved quality of life throughout their production cycles, with reduced stress, disease, and mortality
